text: Which statement best defines a hydrogen bond in biological systems?
choices: A strong covalent bond formed by sharing electrons equally between two carbon atoms
A weak attraction between a partially positive hydrogen and a partially negative atom such as oxygen or nitrogen
A bond formed when one atom transfers a proton to another atom
A permanent ionic bond between hydrogen and oxygen in water
explain: Hydrogen bonds are weak intermolecular attractions between a partially positive hydrogen and an electronegative atom (O, N).

text: Why is water considered a polar molecule?
choices: It has equal sharing of electrons and no partial charges
It has a symmetrical shape that cancels all charges
Oxygen pulls shared electrons closer, giving oxygen a partial negative charge and the hydrogens partial positive charges
Water contains ionic bonds that fully separate charges
explain: Oxygen is more electronegative and pulls electrons toward itself, creating partial charges and molecular polarity.

text: Which property of water is most directly caused by hydrogen bonding between water molecules?
choices: Water has a low heat capacity
Water molecules repel each other strongly
Water can dissolve all substances equally well
Water has cohesion, allowing molecules to stick to each other
explain: Cohesion (water molecules sticking to each other) results from hydrogen bonding among water molecules.

text: What does cohesion mean?
choices: Water sticking to other polar surfaces
Water resisting changes in temperature
Water molecules sticking to each other
Water breaking apart into hydrogen and oxygen
explain: Cohesion is the attraction between molecules of the same substance (water molecules sticking together).

text: What does adhesion mean?
choices: Attraction between water molecules and other substances
Attraction between amino acids in a protein
Attraction between identical water molecules only
Loss of heat through evaporation
explain: Adhesion is attraction between molecules of different substances, e.g., water and plant cell walls.

text: Why does water have a high specific heat?
choices: Its hydrogen bonds absorb energy before the molecules speed up
Water molecules are nonpolar and store thermal energy easily
Water cannot gain kinetic energy
Water contains carbon, which stabilizes temperature
explain: Hydrogen bonds require energy to break, so water absorbs heat before its temperature rises significantly.

text: Which best describes a monomer?
choices: A large molecule made of many smaller repeating units
A small building block that can join with others to make a larger molecule
A type of bond that holds lipids together
A protein made of one amino acid
explain: Monomers are the small subunits that join to form polymers (large molecules).

text: Which pair correctly matches a macromolecule with one of its monomers?
choices: Protein — nucleotide
Nucleic acid — monosaccharide
Carbohydrate — monosaccharide
Lipid — amino acid
explain: Carbohydrates are built from monosaccharide monomers (e.g., glucose).

text: Which statement about lipids is most accurate?
choices: Lipids are true polymers made from repeating identical monomers
Lipids are generally hydrophobic because they contain many nonpolar hydrocarbon regions
Lipids dissolve easily in water because they are highly charged
Lipids are made only of nitrogen and phosphorus
explain: Lipids have large nonpolar hydrocarbon regions that make them hydrophobic.

text: What type of monomer makes up proteins?
choices: Fatty acids
Nucleotides
Amino acids
Monosaccharides
explain: Proteins are polymers of amino acid monomers.

text: What is the primary structure of a protein?
choices: The overall three-dimensional shape of one polypeptide
The sequence of amino acids in the polypeptide chain
The interaction between multiple polypeptide subunits
The local folding into alpha helices and beta pleated sheets
explain: Primary structure is the linear sequence of amino acids in a polypeptide.

text: Which statement correctly describes nucleotides?
choices: They are the monomers of proteins
They contain a phosphate group, a sugar, and a nitrogenous base
They are used only in RNA and not DNA
They are held together in proteins by peptide bonds
explain: Nucleotides consist of a phosphate, a sugar (ribose or deoxyribose), and a nitrogenous base.

text: A scientist compares two beakers placed under identical lamps. One contains water and the other contains ethanol. After 20 minutes, the ethanol has increased in temperature much more than the water. Which explanation is best?
choices: Water has more dissolved ions, so it always warms faster
Water’s hydrogen bonds allow it to absorb more heat before its temperature rises significantly
Ethanol is polar, so it cannot absorb heat
Water molecules are heavier, so they never move faster
explain: Water's hydrogen bonds give it a high specific heat, so it resists temperature change compared to ethanol.

text: A student says, ‘Cellulose and starch are both made of glucose, so they must have the same structure and function.’ Which response is best?
choices: The student is correct because identical monomers always produce identical polymers
The student is incorrect because the arrangement of glucose monomers can differ, producing molecules with different structures and functions
The student is incorrect because starch is made of amino acids, while cellulose is made of glucose
The student is correct because all carbohydrates are used only for short-term energy
explain: Different glycosidic linkages between glucose monomers produce distinct structures (e.g., starch vs. cellulose) with different functions.

text: A mutation changes one amino acid in a protein from a nonpolar amino acid to a charged amino acid in the protein’s interior. What is the most likely result?
choices: The protein’s primary structure stays the same, so its shape will definitely not change
The protein may fold differently because interactions among side chains help determine tertiary structure
The protein will automatically become a lipid
The protein will gain more nucleotides and become longer
explain: A charged residue in the hydrophobic interior can disrupt hydrophobic interactions and alter folding and function.

text: Which statement best explains why carbon is so useful in biological molecules?
choices: Carbon can form up to four covalent bonds, allowing diverse and stable molecular structures
Carbon always forms ionic bonds in living organisms
Carbon can bond only to oxygen, which limits molecular variety
Carbon is chemically inert, so it does not react much
explain: Carbon's four valence electrons allow it to make diverse and stable structures (chains, rings, branches).

text: A researcher heats a protein and observes that the protein loses function but its amino acid sequence remains unchanged. Which level of protein structure was most directly disrupted?
choices: Primary structure
Secondary and tertiary structure
The phospholipid structure
DNA base pairing
explain: Heat disrupts hydrogen bonds and other interactions that maintain secondary and tertiary folding, leading to loss of function.

text: A biologist adds water to a disaccharide and observes that it breaks into two monosaccharides. Which process occurred?
choices: Dehydration synthesis
Hydrolysis
Peptide bonding
Denaturation
explain: Hydrolysis uses water to break covalent bonds, splitting polymers into monomers.

text: Two DNA samples are compared. Sample 1 has more hydrogen bonds between strands than Sample 2. Which conclusion is most supported?
choices: Sample 1 likely contains a higher proportion of adenine-thymine pairs
Sample 1 likely contains a higher proportion of guanine-cytosine pairs
Sample 1 must be RNA instead of DNA
Sample 1 cannot replicate
explain: G-C pairs form three hydrogen bonds (vs. two for A-T), so higher G-C content increases total hydrogen bonding.

text: A student builds a model molecule containing glycerol and three fatty acid tails. What macromolecule has the student modeled?
choices: Phospholipid
Triglyceride
Polypeptide
Nucleotide
explain: A triglyceride consists of glycerol bonded to three fatty acids.

text: A certain molecule is described as having both a hydrophilic region and a hydrophobic region. Which statement best explains why this matters in biology?
choices: Such molecules can interact with both water and nonpolar substances, which helps them perform specialized structural roles
Such molecules cannot participate in any chemical interactions
Such molecules are always proteins
Such molecules are completely soluble in oil but never interact with water
explain: Amphipathic molecules (e.g., phospholipids) have regions that interact with both polar and nonpolar environments, enabling membrane formation.

text: A solution becomes more acidic. Which immediate change must have occurred?
choices: The concentration of OH- increased
The concentration of H+ decreased
The concentration of H+ increased
The number of water molecules became zero
explain: Acidity increases as hydrogen ion (H+) concentration increases.

text: Students place equal volumes of water and vegetable oil onto separate pieces of wax paper. The water droplet beads up, while the oil spreads out more. Which conclusion is best supported by the observation?
choices: Water molecules strongly attract each other, while oil molecules do not show the same degree of cohesion on the wax surface
Water is nonpolar and therefore repelled by wax paper
Oil has stronger hydrogen bonding than water
Wax paper dissolves in water but not oil
explain: Water's cohesion (hydrogen bonding) causes droplets to bead; oil's weaker intermolecular attractions allow it to spread.
experiment: Equal volumes of water and vegetable oil were placed on wax paper; water formed beads while oil spread, indicating differences in cohesion/adhesion.

text: A researcher studies two proteins that differ by only one amino acid. Protein X functions normally at body temperature, but Protein Y unfolds more easily when exposed to mild heat. Which hypothesis is most reasonable?
choices: Protein Y likely has a change in an amino acid that disrupts interactions important for maintaining structure
Protein Y must have lost all peptide bonds
Protein X is not a protein because it remains folded
Protein Y became a carbohydrate after the mutation
explain: A single amino acid substitution can weaken stabilizing interactions (hydrophobic packing, hydrogen bonds), making the protein less heat-stable.
experiment: Two homologous proteins were compared under mild heat; Protein Y lost function more rapidly, suggesting reduced structural stability.

text:
In a lab, students test how quickly different liquids change temperature when heated for 5 minutes. Their results are shown below:

Liquid A: temperature increases by 18°C
Liquid B: temperature increases by 7°C

Which inference is best supported?
choices: Liquid A has a higher specific heat than Liquid B
Liquid B likely requires more energy to raise its temperature, indicating a higher specific heat
Liquid B cannot absorb thermal energy
Liquid A must contain more hydrogen bonds than Liquid B
explain: A smaller temperature change under equal heating suggests Liquid B has a higher specific heat (requires more energy per degree).
experiment: Equal heating was applied to two liquids and temperature change was recorded; Liquid B changed less, indicating higher specific heat.

text: A DNA sequence mutation changes one codon so that the resulting amino acid has a side chain with a very different charge. The altered amino acid is located on the outer surface of the protein, where it normally interacts with water. Which prediction is most reasonable?
choices: The mutation could change how the protein interacts with surrounding water or other molecules, potentially affecting function
The mutation cannot matter because only interior amino acids affect protein function
The mutation proves the DNA can no longer replicate
The protein will definitely become longer
explain: Surface residues mediate interactions; changing charge can alter binding or solubility and thus function.
experiment: A surface-exposed residue was mutated and the protein's interaction with solvent and partners was tested; altered interactions were observed.

text:
Students compare the pH of three solutions:
Solution 1: pH 3
Solution 2: pH 7
Solution 3: pH 10

Which conclusion is correct?
choices: Solution 3 has the highest hydrogen ion concentration
Solution 2 is acidic because it contains equal H+ and OH-
Solution 1 is the most acidic and has the greatest hydrogen ion concentration
Solution 3 is neutral because it has the highest pH
explain: Lower pH corresponds to higher [H+]; pH 3 is most acidic among the three.
experiment: Three solutions were measured for pH; numeric pH values indicate relative H+ concentrations.

text: A scientist compares two carbohydrates. Both are made entirely of glucose monomers. One is easily digested by humans, while the other is not. Which explanation best fits the data?
choices: Humans can digest only carbohydrates made of fructose
The carbohydrates likely differ in the type or orientation of bonds linking the glucose monomers
One carbohydrate must actually be a protein
The indigestible carbohydrate cannot contain carbon
explain: Different glycosidic linkages (e.g., alpha vs beta) affect enzyme recognition and digestibility.
experiment: Enzymatic digestion assays showed one polymer was cleaved by human enzymes while the other resisted digestion.

text: A class tests the effect of pH on the shape of an egg white protein solution. At extreme pH values, the proteins clump and lose their normal structure. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Changes in pH can disrupt interactions that help maintain protein shape
pH changes alter the nucleotide sequence of the protein
pH changes always break every peptide bond in a protein
Proteins function best only at pH 0
explain: Altering pH changes side-chain charges, disrupting interactions that stabilize folding, causing denaturation.
experiment: Egg white proteins were exposed to different pH levels and structural loss and aggregation were observed at extreme pH.
